macro_segments.title: Introduction and Overview of Nachum HaMadi's Opinions

micro_segments.title: Opening remarks and context from Daf 7 Amud 2
Nachum HaMadi's ruling on business prohibition: one day before holidays only
Identity and background of Nachum HaMadi as a Tanna from Madai
Rabbinic rejection of Nachum HaMadi's opinions: 'nishtaka hadavar velo ne'emar'

macro_segments.title: Nachum HaMadi's Additional Rejected Opinions on Animals and Tithes

micro_segments.title: Nachum's opinion on selling male old horses to non-Jews during war
Reference to Mishnah on Daf Yud Daled Amud Bet regarding animal sales
Comparison with Ben Beteira's opinion on horse sales and gender distinctions
Nachum's opinion on tithing the shevet plant (seeds, leaves, stems)
Distinction between Nachum's wild plant and Rabbi Eliezer's garden variety regarding tithing

macro_segments.title: Rav Acha bar Manyumi's Qualification and the One Accepted Nachum Opinion

micro_segments.title: Rav Acha bar Manyumi's challenge to the blanket rejection of Nachum HaMadi
One Nachum opinion accepted: requesting personal needs during shomea tefilla

macro_segments.title: Dispute between Rabbi Eliezer and Rabbi Yehoshua on Prayer Structure

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Eliezer's view: request personal needs first, then pray the Shemoneh Esrei
Rabbi Yehoshua's view: pray first, then request personal needs
Both rabbis' attempts to reconcile opposing biblical verses supporting their views
Rav Simlai's ruling: praise God first, then ask for personal needs
Example from Moshe's prayer in Deuteronomy establishing the order
Dispute over learning the prayer order from Moshe
Rabbi Eliezer's objection: Moshe was exceptional and unique

macro_segments.title: Halachic Rulings on When and Where to Insert Personal Requests in Prayer

micro_segments.title: Chachamim's middle position: personal requests during shomea tefilla blessing
Rav Yehuda's halacha: request personal needs in shomea tefilla blessing
Rav Yehuda bar Rav Shmuel's addition: can add relevant requests at end of each blessing
Rav Chiya bar Ashi's specification: add health prayers in health blessing, sustenance in rain blessing
Rav Yehoshua ben Levi's allowance: longer personal requests after the Amidah

macro_segments.title: Public Holidays of Non-Jews and the Prohibition on Business

micro_segments.title: Mishnah listing the holidays of idol worshippers: Kalenda, Saturnalia, Cartisim, Yom Genosya, coronation and death of kings
Chachamim's ruling: death celebrations only forbidden if accompanied by burning of idolatrous items
Private celebrations: shaving day, return from sea voyage, release from prison, son's celebration—only that day and person forbidden

macro_segments.title: Explanation of Kalenda and Saturnalia: Connection to Winter Solstice

micro_segments.title: Rav Chanan bar Rava: Kalenda is eight days after tekufat Tevet; Saturnalia is eight days before
Midrash on Adam HaRishon witnessing the winter solstice and establishing eight-day celebrations
Two positions on when Adam was created: Tishrei vs. Nisan and their implications

macro_segments.title: Alternative Account: Adam's Reaction to First Sunset and Sacrifice

micro_segments.title: Adam's fear upon first sunset and his and Chavah's weeping
Adam's recognition at dawn and his sacrifice of a special ox
Rav Yehuda's tradition: the ox had a single horn in its forehead

macro_segments.title: Rome's Kalenda Festival and the Status of Dependent Towns

micro_segments.title: Rav Matna's question: towns dependent on Rome that celebrate Kalenda—are they forbidden or permitted?
Rabbi Yehoshua ben Levi vs. Rabbi Yochanan on Kalenda prohibition scope
Halacha follows Rabbi Yochanan: only actual worshippers of the holiday are forbidden

macro_segments.title: Jews Outside the Land of Israel and Inadvertent Idolatry at Non-Jewish Celebrations

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Yishmael's teaching: diaspora Jews engage in idol worship unwittingly (betahara)
Case: idolater's wedding feast with Jewish guests eating their own food—considered as if eating idolatrous offerings
Proof from Exodus: 'vekara lecha ve'achalta mizivcho'—invitation itself creates complicity

macro_segments.title: Thirty-Day Rule for Post-Celebration Meals and Extended Duration of Prohibition

micro_segments.title: Halacha: thirty days after a celebration, meals are forbidden whether explicitly connected or not
Beyond thirty days: forbidden only if the idolater explicitly mentions the celebration, otherwise permitted
Rav Papa: prohibition extends up to twelve months if explicitly mentioned; begins when preparations start
Case of Rav Yitzchak bar Rav Mesharsheya: withdrew from a feast after twelve months due to perceived idol worship
Gemara's leniency: Rav Yitzchak was an important person (adam chashuv) and his withdrawal had different implications

macro_segments.title: Cartisim and the Two Conquests of Rome; Negotiation Parable

micro_segments.title: Rav Yehuda: Cartisim is the day Rome became the ruling empire
Rav Yosef: Rome had two conquests—one against Cleopatra and one against the Greeks
Rav Dimi: Romans fought thirty-two battles against Greeks before partnering with Jews
Parable of the pearl, precious stone, onyx, and Torah scroll—Rome's supremacy over Greece secured by the Jews and Torah
Biblical connection: partnership parallel to Jacob and Esau; transition from 'I will go with you' to 'Let my lord pass before his servant'

macro_segments.title: Historical Account of Roman Rule and Suppression of Rabbinic Smicha

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Yishmael bar Yosi's teaching: 180 years before the Temple's destruction, Rome trampled Israel
Eighty years before destruction: sages decreed tumah on the diaspora and glass vessels
Forty years before destruction: Sanhedrin relocated from Lishkas HaGazis to Chanot
Significance: Sanhedrin could no longer judge criminal cases (dinei kenasos) without proper authority
Rome's persecution: decree to execute anyone teaching or receiving rabbinic smicha; Rabbi Yehuda ben Bava's heroism to preserve smicha

topical_tags.term: Rabbi Eliezer vs. Rabbi Yehoshua prayer order
Rabbi Yehoshua ben Levi vs. Rabbi Yochanan
Jacob and Esau as Rome and Persia parallel
Rabbi Yishmael bar Yosi historical chronology
